Kernel from Tr(|p| ζ |p|^{-1} ζ) at d=6 and point evaluation

Let H=L^2(ℝ^d). For a=1,...,d define the standard position and momentum operators by
  (x̂_aψ)(x)=x_aψ(x),     p̂_a=-i∂_a,
and define |p̂|=(p̂_ap̂_a)^{1/2}. Let ζ(x)=2(4-d)/|x|^2 and let ζ̂ be multiplication by ζ(x).

Fix the Fourier convention ⟨x|k⟩=e^{ik \cdot x} and ∫_{ℝ^d} d^dk/(2\pi)^d |k⟩⟨k|=I. Consider the (regularized) trace expression
  E = (1/4) Tr[ |p̂| ζ̂ |p̂|^{-1} ζ̂ ].

By inserting Fourier and position resolutions of the identity, write E in the form
  E = ∬_{ℝ^d×ℝ^d} dx dy ∬_{ℝ^d×ℝ^d} (dk dq)/(2\pi)^{2d}  \cdot  f(k,q,x,y),
thereby defining a kernel f(k,q,x,y).

Take d=6 and x0=(2,2,2,2,2,2)\inℝ^6. Evaluate f at q=k and x=y=x0 (assume k\neq0 so |k| is defined), and compute
  out = (1/3)  \cdot  ( f(k,k,x0,x0) )^{-1}.
Find out as an exact integer.

Solution.
Step 1 (rewrite_E_as_fourier_integral).
Start from Tr[A]=∫ d^dx ⟨x|A|x⟩ with A=(1/4)|p̂|ζ̂|p̂|^{-1}ζ̂. Insert the momentum and position resolutions:
  I=∫ d^dk/(2\pi)^d |k⟩⟨k|,   I=∫ d^dy |y⟩⟨y|.
Using
  ⟨x||p̂||k⟩ = |k|e^{ik \cdot x},      ⟨y||p̂|^{-1}|q⟩ = (1/|q|)e^{iq \cdot y},
  ⟨k|ζ̂|y⟩ = e^{-ik \cdot y}ζ(y),       ⟨q|ζ̂|x⟩ = e^{-iq \cdot x}ζ(x),
one obtains
  f(k,q,x,y) = (1/4)  \cdot  ζ(x)ζ(y)  \cdot  e^{i(k-q) \cdot (x-y)}  \cdot  (|k|/|q|).

Step 2 (evaluate_at_symmetric_point).
Set q=k and x=y=x0. Then e^{i(k-k) \cdot (x0-x0)}=1 and |k|/|q|=1, so
  f(k,k,x0,x0) = (1/4) ζ(x0)^2,
independent of k (for k\neq0).

Step 3 (compute_zeta_at_point).
Here d=6, so
  ζ(x)=2(4-6)/|x|^2 = -4/|x|^2.
For x0=(2,2,2,2,2,2),
  |x0|^2 = 6 \cdot (2^2) = 24,
hence
  ζ(x0) = -4/24 = -1/6,
so
  f(k,k,x0,x0) = (1/4) \cdot (1/36) = 1/144.

Step 4 (invert_and_scale).
Therefore
  out = (1/3) \cdot (1/144)^{-1} = (1/3) \cdot 144 = 48.

Uniqueness Test
1) The operator expression E and the Fourier convention ⟨x|k⟩=e^{ik \cdot x} uniquely determine the mixed (k,q,x,y) integral representation and hence determine the kernel f(k,q,x,y) uniquely by direct insertion of resolutions of the identity.

2) The substitutions q=k and x=y=x0 are deterministic. For k\neq0, the ratio |k|/|q| equals 1, and the phase factor e^{i(k-q) \cdot (x-y)} equals 1, so f(k,k,x0,x0) reduces uniquely to (1/4)ζ(x0)^2 with no residual dependence on k.

3) The point x0 and dimension d=6 uniquely determine ζ(x0)=2(4-d)/|x0|^2, hence uniquely determine f(k,k,x0,x0)=1/144 and therefore uniquely determine out=(1/3) \cdot 144=48.

48
